one_line_takeaway: Safe RLHF-V ports safety-constrained (Lagrangian min-max) RLHF to multimodal LLMs, contributing the dual-preference BeaverTails-V dataset, the Beaver-Guard-V guardrail, and a budget-bound stabilizer, and shows it can lift both safety and helpfulness where single-objective methods cannot.
key_finding: Safety and helpfulness can be jointly improved in the multimodal setting via decoupled dual-preference data plus constrained optimization, whereas single-dimension methods trade one off against the other. The paper's stated aggregate improvement is +34.2% safety and +34.3% helpfulness. In Table 3 (win rates), Safe RLHF-V is the only method (vs single-dimension RLHF-V / LLaVA-RLHF / DPO / PPO / MM-RLHF) that raises BOTH axes above the 0.5 baseline across all five benchmarks; e.g. LLaVA-1.5-7B on VLGuard reaches 0.852 safety / 0.793 helpful. By contrast single-dimension +DPO(Safety) buys high safety (0.793 on BeaverTails-V) at the cost of helpfulness (0.441), and +DPO(Helpful) does the reverse (0.235 safety / 0.851 helpful). Beaver-Guard-V beats Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision (multi-level accuracy 0.85 vs 0.64, F1 0.88 vs 0.74, FPR 0.20 vs 0.71), and 5 rounds of its filter-and-regenerate raise a precursor model's safety by an average of 40.9%.
core_contribution: Safe RLHF-V is the first framework to port safety-constrained RLHF (previously developed by the same authors for language-only models in Safe RLHF [16]) to the multimodal (image-text) setting. Its three coupled contributions are: (1) BeaverTails-V, an open-source multimodal preference dataset that independently annotates each response pair for helpfulness AND safety (dual/decoupled preferences), plus graded safety meta-labels (minor / moderate / severe) over a taxonomy of 9 primary and 20 secondary harm categories; (2) Beaver-Guard-V, a moderation/guardrail model fine-tuned on those labels that inspects both prompts and generated responses and reject-and-regenerates unsafe outputs; and (3) the Safe RLHF-V algorithm itself, which trains a separate Reward Model-Vision (RM-V, helpfulness) and Cost Model-Vision (CM-V, safety) and optimizes an MLLM under a Lagrangian min-max constrained objective, adding a 'budget bound' projection on the Lagrange multiplier to stabilize the otherwise-unstable multimodal training. Note: RLHF-V, LLaVA-RLHF, and MM-RLHF are prior multimodal-alignment methods used here as BASELINES, not part of this paper's contribution; likewise BeaverTails [15] and PKU-SafeRLHF [45] are the authors' prior language-only datasets that inspire, but are distinct from, BeaverTails-V.
threat_model: MLLMs can be induced to emit harmful content by the image modality in ways less pronounced in text-only LLMs, and safety alignment inherited from a backbone LLM can be eroded by downstream fine-tuning. The adversary supplies harmful or jailbreak-style queries (optionally paired with harm-aligned images) aiming to elicit unsafe responses; the paper measures this via Attack Success Rate (ASR = proportion of unsafe responses, judged by GPT-4o). The defense is twofold: an inference-time guardrail (Beaver-Guard-V) that filters unsafe prompts/responses and regenerates, and a training-time safety constraint (the cost model + Lagrangian min-max) that reduces the policy's probability of producing unsafe outputs while preserving helpfulness.
limitations: Scope is explicitly limited to image-text; other modalities are left to future work (stated in the conclusion). The CM-V (safety) is markedly harder to train than RM-V: only 54.5% accuracy at 5K preference pairs vs RM-V's 82.2%, reaching 79.3% only at 15K, revealing the difficulty of modeling safety constraints. Multimodal constrained optimization is highly unstable and sensitive to the initial Lagrange multiplier lambda_0 (fixed multipliers yield parabola-shaped win rates and can cause model collapse/gibberish), which the budget-bound heuristic mitigates but does not eliminate. All headline metrics are GPT-4o-judged win rates on a self-constructed eval set rather than absolute or human-scored measures, so numbers are relative to the reference model and inherit any GPT-4o judging bias. Data provenance (Yandex image scraping, jailbreak-generated prompts) may raise coverage/quality concerns.

datasets_used: BeaverTails-V (this paper's own dual-preference dataset; ~32k prompts, 9 primary / 20 secondary harm categories, safe + minor/moderate/severe labels; also used as the self-constructed eval set)
MM-SafetyBench (external safety benchmark, incl. SD / SD_TYPO / TYPO splits)
quantitative_results: Stated aggregate improvement (abstract/intro/conclusion, not a single table cell): +34.2% safety, +34.3% helpfulness

Beaver-Guard-V over 5 rounds of filter-and-regenerate: average +40.9% safety on the precursor model; round 5 gives the lowest ASR across all evaluated models (per-round per-model ASR values not reproduced here due to unreliable table extraction)
Table 3 metric = GPT-4o-judged pairwise WIN RATE vs the un-optimized reference model, 0.5 = tie/baseline (so 0.852 means an 85.2% win rate, +0.352 over tie)
Safe RLHF-V, LLaVA-1.5-7B: BeaverTails-V 0.579 safety / 0.770 helpful; VLGuard 0.852 safety / 0.793 helpful; SPA-VL 0.620 / 0.613; MM-SafetyBench 0.720 / 0.774; VLSBench 0.611 / 0.814
Single-dimension contrast on BeaverTails-V (LLaVA-1.5-7B): +DPO(Safety) 0.793 safety / 0.441 helpful; +DPO(Helpful) 0.235 safety / 0.851 helpful; +PPO(Safety) 0.957 safety / 0.253 helpful

Beaver-Guard-V vs Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision (Table 2a, multi-level): accuracy 0.85 vs 0.64, precision 0.88 vs 0.67, recall 0.87 vs 0.84, F1 0.88 vs 0.74, false-positive rate 0.20 vs 0.71; binary Beaver-Guard-V accuracy 0.78, FPR 0.47
Beaver-Guard-V vs Llama-Guard-3-11B-Vision average accuracy (Table 2b) 0.88 vs 0.68 and average F1 0.92 vs 0.78 across BeaverTails-V / SPA-VL / MM-SafetyBench splits
RM-V vs CM-V accuracy by data size (Table 4): RM-V 82.2% (5K) rising to 86.3% (30K); CM-V 54.5% (5K), 76.6% (10K), 79.3% (15K), up to 80.4% (30K); CM sign-accuracy 66.1% (5K) up to 89.7% (30K)

Table 1 ASR delta (text-image minus text-only), showing BeaverTails-V has genuine multimodal integration: BeaverTails-V delta 0.216 (LLaVA-1.5-7B) / 0.240 (Qwen2-VL-7B) vs SPA-VL 0.188 / 0.068 and MM-SafetyBench 0.192 / 0.119
